message:
update(demo,evidence,beads): add narrative metadata to featured spotlight samples

Enrich the 7 featured gallery samples with structured narrative objects
(headline, lookFor, proves) so the spotlight panel renders proof-point
context instead of generic placeholder text. Each featured diagram now
carries justification anchors (J1-J5) linking it to the capability
scenario matrix.

- frankenmermaid_demo_showcase.html: extend sample() with optional
  narrative parameter; wire narrative into spotlight description, "why
  this?" panel, and proof-point badge rendering for flowchart, sequence,
  state, gantt, journey, and gitGraph featured entries.
- evidence/capability_scenario_matrix.json: add narrative_headline,
  narrative_focus, and proof_points fields to the 5 showcase-featured
  scenario entries (flowchart, sequence, state, gantt + 1 more).
- .beads/issues.jsonl: update 3 bead records (lines 87, 114, 156) with
  revised metadata.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1y5.5","title":"Build SVG theming system with presets and customization","description":"Implement a comprehensive theming system for SVG output that provides both preset themes and full customization:

1. THEME PRESETS (matching mermaid-js for compatibility, plus new ones):
   - Default: Clean neutral colors (blue/gray)
   - Dark: Dark background with bright accents
   - Forest: Green/brown earth tones
   - Neutral: Minimal grayscale
   - Corporate: Professional blue/gray/white
   - Neon: Bright neon on dark (new)
   - Pastel: Soft muted colors (new)
   - High-Contrast: WCAG AA compliant (new)
   - Monochrome: Black and white only (new)
   - Blueprint: White-on-blue technical drawing style (new)

2. CSS-BASED THEMING: Embed a <style> element in SVG with CSS custom properties (variables). Users can override via external CSS. Variables: --fm-bg, --fm-node-fill, --fm-node-stroke, --fm-edge-color, --fm-text-color, --fm-cluster-fill, --fm-accent-1 through --fm-accent-8.

3. PER-NODE STYLING: Support class-based and inline style overrides from IR (classDef in mermaid syntax). Apply as CSS classes on SVG elements.

4. COLOR PALETTE GENERATION: Given a base color, auto-generate a harmonious palette for node fills. Use HSL rotation for distinct-but-cohesive colors.

5. FONT CONFIGURATION: Configurable font-family, font-size, font-weight. Default: system font stack. Embed font-face if web font URL provided.

 {"id":"bd-1y5.6","title":"Add accessibility features to SVG output","description":"Make SVG diagrams accessible to screen readers and keyboard navigation:

1. ARIA ATTRIBUTES: role=img on root SVG, aria-label with diagram description. Nodes get role=graphics-symbol, edges get role=graphics-symbol.

2. TITLE/DESC: SVG <title> element with diagram type and summary. <desc> element with full text description of the diagram content (auto-generated from IR).

3. TEXT ALTERNATIVES: Every node and edge gets a <title> child with human-readable description. E.g., 'Node A connects to Node B via solid arrow labeled Submit'.

4. KEYBOARD NAVIGATION: tabindex on interactive elements. data-fm-node-id and data-fm-edge-id attributes for JS focus management.

5. HIGH CONTRAST MODE: Detect prefers-contrast media query in embedded CSS. Switch to high-contrast palette automatically.

6. REDUCED MOTION: Detect prefers-reduced-motion. Disable any CSS animations on edges.

This ensures FrankenMermaid diagrams are usable by everyone, which is a significant differentiator.

 {"id":"bd-20fq","title":"EPIC: Incremental Subgraph Re-Layout (§6.1)","description":"Only recompute changed subgraphs on edit. Currently the full layout is recomputed on any change, which is O(V+E) regardless of change size. This epic implements Salsa-style dependency tracking at the subgraph level: mark dirty subgraphs on edit, re-evaluate only those, leaving stable subgraphs untouched.

## Motivation
For interactive editing of large diagrams (1000+ nodes), full layout recompute on every keystroke is prohibitively expensive. Incremental subgraph re-layout achieves O(delta) work proportional to the size of the change, not the size of the entire diagram. This is the single most transformative optimization for FrankenMermaid interactive editing story.

## Approach — Salsa-style Dependency Tracking
- Build a dependency graph that maps each subgraph region to the layout inputs it depends on (node positions, edge connectivity, rank assignments).
- On edit, walk the dependency graph to identify which subgraphs are invalidated (dirty).
- Re-run layout only on dirty subgraphs, stitching results back into the global layout.
- Verify layout equivalence: incremental result must be bit-identical to full recompute result for the same input.

## Key Risks
- Dependency graph maintenance overhead could exceed savings for small diagrams (mitigate: bypass for <50 nodes).
- Stitching subgraph layouts back together may introduce seam artifacts at subgraph boundaries (mitigate: boundary overlap zones).
- Correctness verification: must prove incremental == full recompute via property tests.

## Relationship to Existing Work
- Complements bd-2u0.7 (incremental pipeline for WASM editing) but operates at a lower level — subgraph-level layout invalidation rather than pipeline-stage caching.
- Depends on fm-layout crate extraction being complete.

## Graveyard Reference: §6.1 — Incremental Subgraph Re-Layout
Score: 6.7 (Transformative). Tier A.

## Success Metrics
- Layout update latency < 10ms for single-node edits on 1000-node diagrams.
- Bit-identical output vs full recompute for all test cases.
- Cache hit rate > 80% for typical editing patterns.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:21:24.167882862Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:23:06.756368126Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","incremental","layout","performance"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-20fq","depends_on_id":"bd-3bc.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:23:06.756313564Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-20fq.1","title":"Design dependency graph for subgraph-level layout invalidation","description":"Design the dependency graph data structure that tracks which layout computations depend on which input fragments.

## Deliverables
1. A formal specification of the dependency graph schema: nodes represent subgraph regions (connected components or user-defined subgraph boundaries), edges represent data dependencies (e.g., subgraph A rank assignment depends on subgraph B edge connectivity).
2. Granularity decision: define what constitutes a 'subgraph region' — options include (a) Mermaid subgraph declarations, (b) connected components after removing cut vertices, (c) fixed-size spatial partitions. Document tradeoffs of each.
3. API contract: given an edit (node add/remove/move, edge add/remove), the dependency graph must return the minimal set of dirty subgraph IDs in O(log N) time.
4. Memory budget: dependency graph overhead must be < 10% of the layout data structure size.
5. Integration plan: how the dependency graph hooks into the existing fm-layout Sugiyama pipeline.

## Salsa Inspiration
Salsa (used in rust-analyzer) tracks fine-grained dependencies between queries. Here we adapt that concept: each subgraph layout is a 'query' whose inputs are the nodes/edges/ranks in that region. When an input changes, only queries that transitively depend on it are invalidated.

## Output
- Design document (doc comment or ADR) checked into fm-layout crate.
- Rust trait definitions for DependencyGraph, SubgraphRegion, DirtySet.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:21:37.883267262Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T03:21:06.698719913Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","design","incremental","layout"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-20fq.1","depends_on_id":"bd-20fq","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:21:37.883267262Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-2nw.1","title":"Extract diff rendering from mermaid_diff.rs into fm-render-term","description":"# Diff Rendering Extraction

Extract the complete diff rendering functionality from FrankenTUI's mermaid_diff.rs (1,830 lines) into frankenmermaid's fm-render-term crate.

## Reference Code
- Source: /dp/frankentui/crates/ftui-extras/src/mermaid_diff.rs
- Lines: 1,830
- Purpose: Side-by-side diagram comparison with change highlighting

## Key Features to Extract

### 1. Diff Computation
- Node-level diff (added, removed, modified, unchanged)
- Edge-level diff (new connections, removed connections, changed labels)
- Cluster/subgraph diff
- Attribute diff (shapes, styles, classes)

### 2. Visual Representation
- Side-by-side layout
- Inline unified diff
- Change markers (color coding)
- Structural diff highlighting

### 3. Output Modes
- Terminal diff with ANSI colors
- Plain text diff (for CI/logging)
- Structured diff (JSON for tooling)

## Implementation Notes

The FrankenTUI diff system uses:
- LCS (Longest Common Subsequence) for sequence alignment
- Edit distance for similarity scoring
- Semantic grouping to minimize visual noise

This is NOT a blind copy - we must:
1. Remove ftui-specific dependencies
2. Use fm-core IR types directly
3. Integrate with fm-layout for positioning
4. Add comprehensive tests

## Acceptance Criteria

- All diff modes from reference implemented
- Unit tests for each diff algorithm
- Integration tests with real diagram pairs
- Performance within 2x of reference
- Deterministic output (same input pair => same diff)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:15:10.155700771Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-18T07:24:47.432163678Z","closed_at":"2026-03-18T07:24:47.431416113Z","close_reason":"Diff rendering extracted into fm-render-term/src/diff.rs (27KB).  {"id":"bd-2nw.14","title":"Implement advanced edge routing with orthogonal and spline paths","description":"# Advanced Edge Routing

Implement sophisticated edge routing algorithms from FrankenTUI to support orthogonal routing, curved edges, and proper edge-node avoidance.

## Reference Code
- Source: /dp/frankentui/crates/ftui-extras/src/diagram_layout.rs
- Lines: ~800 (routing section)
- Purpose: High-quality edge paths

## Current State

fm-layout has basic edge routing but lacks:
- Orthogonal (right-angle) routing
- Node avoidance
- Edge-edge avoidance
- Port assignment
- Curved edge smoothing

## Key Features to Implement

### 1. Orthogonal Router

**Algorithm**: Visibility graph + A* pathfinding

Steps:
1. Build obstacle grid (nodes as rectangles)
2. Compute visibility graph
3. Find shortest orthogonal path
4. Apply bend minimization
5. Assign edge channels

**Properties:**
- O(n²) visibility computation
- O(e log n) pathfinding per edge
- Guaranteed to find path if exists

### 2. Edge Channel Assignment

When multiple edges share a path segment:
1. Compute channel requirements
2. Assign channels to minimize crossings
3. Apply channel-specific offsets
4. Preserve edge ordering

### 3. Port Assignment

For nodes with multiple edges:
1. Group edges by direction
2. Assign port positions
3. Minimize edge crossings at ports
4. Handle multi-edge bundles

### 4. Curved Edge Smoothing

Convert orthogonal paths to smooth curves:
1. Identify corner points
2. Compute Bezier control points
3. Generate cubic Bezier segments
4. Preserve tangent continuity

### 5. Node Avoidance

Ensure edges don't cross through nodes:
1. Detect potential crossings
2. Route around obstacles
3. Minimize detour length
4. Handle nested clusters

### 6. Long Edge Handling

For edges spanning multiple layers:
1. Create dummy nodes at each layer
2. Route through dummies
3. Align dummy nodes for straightness
4. Handle wide nodes

## Rendering Integration

Edge paths output as:
```rust
pub enum EdgePath {
    Straight { from: Point, to: Point },
    Orthogonal { segments: Vec<(Point, Point)> },
    Bezier { segments: Vec<BezierCubic> },
}
```

## Performance Targets

| Graph Size | Routing Time |
|------------|--------------|
| 100 nodes | <50ms |
| 500 nodes | <200ms |
| 1000 nodes | <500ms |

## Acceptance Criteria

- Orthogonal routing working
- No edge-node intersections
- Port assignment minimizing crossings
- Curved edges option available
- Visual parity with FrankenTUI
- Performance within targets","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:19:07.733066831Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-19T18:05:27.757112707Z","closed_at":"2026-03-19T18:05:27.756626616Z","close_reason":"Implemented node-aware orthogonal edge routing: edges now detect when midpoint segments intersect intermediate node bounding boxes and reroute around them.
